note: Data model for the standalone comparative-theology site. Two layers: (1) a doctrine matrix at the confessional-FAMILY level, and (2) a finer set of traditions/movements each carrying coordinates on several independent axes, so any single spectrum or any 2-axis map can be drawn from the same data. Each family/tradition is represented from its own authoritative voice. Positions are 0-100 on each axis (0 = left label, 100 = right label). These are v1 placements for Woods to refine.

axes.label: extent of the atonement
axes.note: This axis asks for whom Christ died. The 'limited / particular' pole holds that his death was intended to secure the elect; the 'unlimited' pole holds that he died for everyone, though only those who believe receive its benefit. A middle position — 'sufficient for all, efficient for the elect' (Amyraldianism) — holds both at once: the death is sufficient for all, and savingly applied to those who come to faith.

axes.label: baptism
axes.note: The infant-baptizing traditions also baptize adults who come to faith, on their profession — so the left pole marks that infants are included, not that believers are excluded. The right pole is the position that baptizes professing believers only, and never infants.

axes.label: the Spirit’s gifts
axes.note: These are the miraculous 'sign' gifts — speaking in tongues, prophecy, and healing (1 Corinthians 12–14) — not spiritual gifts in general like teaching or mercy, which every tradition affirms. The 'ceased' pole holds that the sign gifts belonged to the apostolic age and are no longer given now that the Church is founded and Scripture complete; the 'for today' pole holds that they continue in the church's life. The middle, 'open but cautious,' neither seeks them out nor rules them out.

cells.Catholic: Scripture and Tradition together, interpreted by the Magisterium
cells.Orthodox: Scripture within Holy Tradition, read with the mind of the Church
cells.Anglican: Scripture supreme, read alongside tradition and reason
cells.Lutheran: sola scriptura; the norm of norms, creeds subordinate
cells.Wesleyan/Methodist: Scripture primary, read with tradition, reason, and experience
cells.Reformed: sola scriptura; Scripture the final judge over all tradition
cells.Baptist: sola scriptura; strong biblicism and individual soul competency
cells.Pentecostal: sola scriptura, with a lively expectation of the Spirit still speaking

detail.Catholic: The Catholic Church receives Scripture as the inspired Word of God, yet insists it does not stand alone: as Dei Verbum teaches, Scripture and Tradition flow from one divine wellspring and are honored with equal devotion. Because the Word was entrusted to a living community before a page was written, its authentic interpretation belongs to the Magisterium, the teaching office of pope and bishops. A common misreading pictures Rome placing Church above Bible; the Catechism instead makes the Magisterium a servant that hands on only what it has received, one deposit of faith.
detail.Orthodox: For the Orthodox, Scripture is the crown and heart of Holy Tradition, not a separate authority beside it; the Bible is the Church's own book, born from her life and read within it. Tradition here means not human customs added to the Word but the continuous life of the Spirit across the ages, the Spirit who inspired the Scriptures illumining their reading. So the faithful interpret with the mind of the Church, guided by the Fathers, councils, and liturgy, not by private judgment. Thus an Orthodox reader distrusts Scripture severed from the community that received and canonized it.
detail.Anglican: Anglicanism holds Scripture as supreme: the Thirty-nine Articles declare that Holy Scripture contains all things necessary to salvation, so nothing beyond it may be required as an article of faith. Yet this supremacy is exercised through careful reading, with tradition and reason as ministering aids rather than rival authorities, a pattern often traced to Richard Hooker. It is not a tidy three-legged stool of equal parts, as later readers sometimes overstate; Scripture judges, while tradition and reason help the Church read it. The family ranges, from Anglo-Catholic to evangelical, over tradition's weight, yet keeps the Bible first.
detail.Lutheran: Lutherans confess sola scriptura: as the Formula of Concord states, Scripture is the norm of norms, the sole rule by which all teaching and teachers must be judged. The creeds and the confessions gathered in the Book of Concord carry real but subordinate authority, honored because they faithfully echo Scripture rather than adding to it. This guards against the misreading that sola scriptura means the Bible read in isolation; Lutherans read within the creedal tradition while denying it any footing above the Word. The confessional LCMS and the ELCA differ on method, yet both keep Scripture supreme.
detail.Wesleyan/Methodist: Wesley called himself a man of one book, and Methodism keeps Scripture primary, the sufficient rule of faith and the final authority in all things necessary to salvation. Yet Wesley read the Bible with the help of Christian tradition, sound reason, and the confirming witness of experience, a pattern later named the Wesleyan Quadrilateral. That image is easily misread as four equal authorities; in Wesley's practice Scripture holds primacy, while tradition, reason, and experience are means by which the Spirit opens its meaning. The aim is a living, warm-hearted reading, never a Bible set against mind or community.
detail.Reformed: The Reformed tradition presses sola scriptura rigorously: as the Westminster Confession affirms, Scripture is the supreme judge over all councils, Fathers, and traditions, and it interprets itself. Its authority rests not on the Church's endorsement but on God its author, sealed to the believer by the inward witness of the Spirit. A frequent misreading imagines this leaves each reader to a private Bible; in fact the confessions prize creeds and the Church's teaching as genuine, though subordinate and correctable, helps. So confessions like Westminster, Belgic, and Heidelberg guide churches only as they conform to Scripture.
detail.Baptist: Baptists are marked by a strong biblicism: Scripture is the sole written authority for faith and practice, as the Baptist Faith and Message and older confessions like the Second London affirm. Alongside this stands soul competency, the conviction that every believer is competent and responsible to read and obey Scripture without a mediating priesthood. That freedom gives the family real range, from confessions leaning Reformed to congregations stressing individual conscience, and bodies as varied as the Southern Baptist Convention and Progressive National Baptist Convention. Yet historic Baptists tie it to accountability under the text and the gathered church.
detail.Pentecostal: Pentecostals stand firmly on sola scriptura, holding the Bible to be the inspired, infallible, authoritative Word of God and all-sufficient rule for faith and conduct, as the Assemblies of God's statement declares. What distinguishes them is a lively expectation that the same Spirit who inspired Scripture still speaks today through prophecy, tongues, and revelation. This is no rival authority: genuine Pentecostal teaching insists every fresh utterance be tested and judged by the written Word, which alone remains the final norm. The family ranges from classical Pentecostals to charismatic and independent streams, guarding against experience overriding Scripture.

cells.Catholic: wounded by sin but able to cooperate with grace
cells.Orthodox: ancestral sin wounds the will but does not destroy its freedom
cells.Anglican: the Articles affirm depravity; prevenient grace restores response
cells.Lutheran: total depravity; the will is bound until regeneration
cells.Wesleyan/Methodist: depravity is real, but prevenient grace frees the will to respond
cells.Reformed: total depravity; the will is in bondage
cells.Baptist: varies: general Baptists free, Reformed Baptists bound
cells.Pentecostal: free to respond; conversion is a genuine decision

detail.Catholic: Catholic teaching, following Trent and the Catechism, holds that original sin wounded human nature and weakened the will, but did not extinguish freedom or leave it wholly corrupt. Grace takes the initiative and heals, yet the restored will genuinely cooperates: God's grace awakens assent, and the person is free to consent or refuse. This is why the tradition can speak of merit — not earning salvation from nothing, but grace crowning its own gifts within a free response. Even the freedom to say yes is itself God's gift.
detail.Orthodox: The Orthodox East frames the human condition around ancestral sin rather than inherited guilt: what Adam passed on is mortality and a will bent toward corruption, not a legal debt shared by all. The will is genuinely wounded and enfeebled, yet its freedom and self-determination remain intact, for the divine image cannot be erased. Salvation is therefore synergy — the Spirit's grace and the person's free response working together toward union with God. Orthodoxy resists any account of total inability, insisting that God never coerces.
detail.Anglican: The Thirty-nine Articles speak plainly of a fallen condition: Article IX confesses original sin, and Article X declares that we have no power to do good works pleasing to God without grace preventing — going before — us. Yet that same grace restores the capacity to respond, so the Anglican settlement holds depravity and enabling grace together rather than choosing between them. Some read the Articles in a more Reformed key and others in a more Arminian one, but all confess that good will begins with God's initiative.
detail.Lutheran: Lutheran confession, articulated in the Formula of Concord and Luther's Bondage of the Will, teaches that in things pertaining to salvation the fallen will is not merely weakened but bound, unable to turn to God by its own powers. The unregenerate person can no more choose faith than a corpse can raise itself; conversion is wholly the Spirit's work through Word and sacrament. This is no fatalism about ordinary life, where reason and civil freedom still operate. Once regenerated, the freed will cooperates, though even that willing is the Spirit's gift.
detail.Wesleyan/Methodist: Wesley took human depravity with full seriousness — the natural person, left to himself, is dead in sin and unable to turn to God. But he insisted that no one is ever merely left to himself: prevenient grace, poured out through Christ on all, goes before conversion and restores a measure of freedom, awakening conscience and enabling a real yes or no. So the will is not naturally free but graciously freed, keeping salvation God's work while making the response genuine. Wesley honored grace without erasing responsibility, so that whosoever will may truly come.
detail.Reformed: The Reformed confessions, from the Canons of Dort to Westminster, teach total depravity: sin has corrupted every faculty, including the will, so that the natural person is unable and unwilling to choose God. This bondage is not a loss of the will's activity — people still choose freely according to their nature — but that fallen nature is enslaved to sin until grace intervenes. What frees the will is effectual, regenerating grace, which the tradition describes as monergistic: God alone gives the new heart from which faith springs. A will in bondage cannot liberate itself; only sovereign grace can.
detail.Baptist: The Baptist family genuinely divides here, which is why the cell reads "varies." General (Arminian) Baptists, in the older free-will stream, hold that grace restores the will so that anyone can respond, while Reformed (Particular) Baptists confess total depravity and a will in bondage until regenerating grace acts, as the Second London Confession affirms. Most congregations land somewhere along that spectrum, and the Baptist Faith and Message is broad enough to shelter both. Because Baptists prize local autonomy and reject binding creeds, this remains a live, unresolved conversation across the family.
detail.Pentecostal: Pentecostal churches, shaped largely by the Wesleyan-Holiness and broader Arminian streams, emphasize that conversion is a real decision: the gospel calls, the Spirit convicts, and the hearer genuinely chooses to repent and believe. This rests not on a claim that fallen humanity is naturally able, but on the conviction that God's grace reaches every person and enables a free response, so no one is excluded by decree. The revivalist heart — the altar call, the invitation to come — assumes a will that grace has freed to answer. A minority leans more Reformed, but the dominant voice affirms a freely responding will.

cells.Catholic: infused and cooperated with, mediated through the sacraments
cells.Orthodox: synergy: grace (God's uncreated energies) and freedom cooperate
cells.Anglican: prevenient grace enables; the tradition holds a range
cells.Lutheran: given through the means of grace, and resistible
cells.Wesleyan/Methodist: prevenient and resistible; grace goes before and enables all
cells.Reformed: irresistible and effectual, given to the elect
cells.Pentecostal: resistible; the Spirit empowers, often with Wesleyan-holiness roots

detail.Catholic: For Catholics, grace is a real participation in God's own life, poured into the soul so that the person is genuinely transformed and not merely declared righteous. The Council of Trent taught that this sanctifying grace is infused, and that the justified truly cooperate with it, growing in holiness through freely chosen good works God's grace makes possible. Ordinarily this grace reaches us through the sacraments, which the Catechism calls efficacious signs that confer what they signify. This is not a transaction that earns salvation; the initiative is always God's, and even our cooperation is itself his gift.
detail.Orthodox: The Orthodox East frames salvation as theosis, the person being drawn into communion with God, and grace is the means. Following Gregory Palamas, the tradition distinguishes God's unknowable essence from his uncreated energies: grace is God himself present and active, not a created thing he hands over. Salvation unfolds as synergy, a real cooperation in which divine grace and human freedom work together, though grace always goes first and does the greater part. This is not a bargain between equals; it is the restored freedom of a creature learning again to say yes to the God who never coerces.
detail.Anglican: Anglicanism inherits a Reformation understanding of grace while holding a genuine internal range. The Thirty-nine Articles teach that we cannot turn to God by our own natural strength, and that prevenient grace must go before to enable any good will, echoing an Augustinian emphasis. Yet Anglicans have long divided over how that grace works: some read the Articles in a broadly Reformed key, others in an Arminian one after the Wesleys and later Anglo-Catholic revival. The Book of Common Prayer holds these together in worship rather than resolving them by decree, so the family prays a common grace it describes in more than one voice.
detail.Lutheran: For Lutherans, grace is God's unmerited favor in Christ, received by faith alone and never earned, the heart of the Reformation's discovery. The Book of Concord teaches that this grace comes to us through concrete means: the preached Word and the sacraments of Baptism and the Lord's Supper, where God actually delivers forgiveness rather than merely picturing it. Lutherans deny that fallen humans cooperate in their own conversion, giving all glory to God, yet they equally insist that grace can be resisted and even lost, refusing to make salvation a coercive decree. The gift is entirely God's, but it is offered to be received, not forced.
detail.Wesleyan/Methodist: For Wesleyans, grace is God's active, unearned love meeting us at every stage, and its signature note is that it goes before. Prevenient grace is given to all people, awakening conscience and restoring enough freedom that anyone can genuinely respond to the gospel. Justifying and sanctifying grace then pardon and progressively heal the believer, so that Wesley could speak of Christian perfection, by which he meant hearts made perfect in love, not a sinless or flawless life. Crucially this grace is resistible: God woos rather than compels, so the human yes is real and no one is excluded in advance.
detail.Reformed: In the Reformed confession, grace is sovereign, effectual, and free, resting salvation wholly on God rather than on anything foreseen in us. Following Augustine and the Canons of Dort, the tradition teaches that saving grace is given to the elect and, when God regenerates the heart, it accomplishes what he intends, which is why it is called irresistible or efficacious. This does not mean people are dragged unwillingly; grace renews the will itself so that the person freely and gladly comes. The point is not divine force but the certainty that God's saving purpose does not fail, and that the glory belongs to him alone.
detail.Baptist: Because Baptists have no single magisterium, grace is one of the family's genuinely contested doctrines, and the poles have historic names. General Baptists in the Arminian line hold that God's enabling grace goes out to all and can be freely accepted or resisted, while Particular Baptists in the Reformed line confess a grace given effectually to the elect. The majority of Baptists today lean toward the resistible, enabling view, and confessions like the Baptist Faith and Message speak of grace broadly enough to shelter both readings. What unites them is insistence on grace alone through faith alone; how that grace operates remains an active, in-house conversation.
detail.Pentecostal: Pentecostals stand largely in the Wesleyan-holiness stream, and so most understand grace as freely offered to all and genuinely resistible, awakening and enabling a response God does not compel. What is distinctive is the accent on the Holy Spirit's ongoing empowerment: grace not only pardons but fills, sanctifies, and equips believers for witness, often expressed in Spirit baptism and spiritual gifts. Statements like the Assemblies of God's fundamental truths hold conversion and continued sanctification together as works of grace. A minority of Pentecostals draw on Reformed convictions, but the movement's dominant voice emphasizes a grace that invites, and can be grieved or refused.

cells.Catholic: predestination held together with real human freedom
cells.Orthodox: grounded in God's foreknowledge, never predestination to damnation
cells.Anglican: Article XVII affirms predestination to life (election), not to damnation; read variously
cells.Lutheran: unconditional election to salvation only, not to damnation
cells.Wesleyan/Methodist: conditional election in view of foreseen faith; whosoever will
cells.Reformed: unconditional election; strict Calvinism adds double predestination
cells.Pentecostal: conditional; the offer is to whosoever

detail.Catholic: The Church holds that God wills the salvation of all and, in eternity, predestines the elect to glory — yet never in a way that overrides human freedom or predestines anyone to sin or damnation. Predestination is read inside the mystery of grace: God's initiative is total, and the person still truly cooperates. Thomists and Molinists dispute how grace and freedom fit together, but both keep the two held together rather than sacrificing either.
detail.Orthodox: The East frames election through God's foreknowledge rather than an eternal decree that fixes each person's destiny in advance. Standing outside time, God knows how each person will freely respond, and elects in accord with that foreseen response. There is no predestination to damnation: God desires everyone to be saved, and the person genuinely shares in salvation through the synergy of grace and freedom.
detail.Anglican: Article XVII affirms predestination to life — God's eternal purpose to bring the elect to salvation in Christ — but says nothing of predestination to damnation, leaving reprobation an open question. Because the Article can be read in a Calvinist, Arminian, or Lutheran key, Anglicanism has carried all three readings within one communion. It also cautions pastorally that the doctrine becomes a snare when pressed further than Scripture plainly warrants.
detail.Lutheran: Lutherans confess election to salvation as God's unconditional gift, grounded wholly in Christ and not in any merit or faith foreseen in the person. But they refuse the symmetrical step of teaching predestination to damnation: the lost are lost by their own rejection of grace, not by a decree against them. This 'single predestination' holds God's sovereignty and human responsibility together without pretending to resolve the tension.
detail.Wesleyan/Methodist: Wesley affirmed predestination but read it as conditional: God elects in view of the faith he foreknows, so the invitation is genuinely open to whosoever will. Prevenient grace goes before every person, restoring the freedom to accept or resist, so no one is shut out by an eternal decree. Election, in this account, is God's gracious foreknowledge meeting a real human response — not an arbitrary choice of some over others.
detail.Reformed: In the Reformed confession, election is unconditional: before the foundation of the world God freely chose those he would save, resting salvation entirely on his sovereign grace rather than on foreseen faith. Strict Calvinism presses this to its symmetrical conclusion — double predestination, in which God also ordains the reprobate to their end — while others affirm election alone and leave the rest to God's justice. Either way, the emphasis falls on grace that owes nothing to the creature.
detail.Baptist: The Baptist family is genuinely split here, which is why the matrix cell reads 'varies.' General (Arminian) Baptists hold conditional election — God chooses those he foreknows will believe — while Particular (Reformed) Baptists confess unconditional election in the Calvinist line. Most Baptist churches fall somewhere along that range, and the question of Calvinism remains one of the family's most active internal debates.
detail.Pentecostal: Pentecostals overwhelmingly hold conditional election: the gospel offer is to whosoever will, and the Spirit draws all who are willing to respond. Rooted largely in the Wesleyan-holiness stream, the movement stresses a genuine human decision and the real openness of salvation to anyone, over any decree that would settle the matter apart from a person's response.

cells.Catholic: sufficient for all, applied through the Church's means
cells.Orthodox: for all; framed less as payment than as Christ the victor and healer
cells.Anglican: sufficient for all; the tradition holds a range
cells.Lutheran: universal in scope, received through faith
cells.Reformed: limited / particular: Christ died for the elect
cells.Pentecostal: unlimited; often includes healing in the atonement

detail.Catholic: The Catholic Church teaches that Christ's sacrifice is of infinite value, more than sufficient to redeem the whole human race, so its saving power reaches every person without exception. The Council of Trent distinguished between what Christ merited for all and what is applied to each soul through faith, conversion, and the Church's sacramental means. This is no stingy limiting of the cross but a conviction that grace, though universally won, must be received and lived. Its sufficiency for all and its efficacy in the believer are truths the tradition holds together.
detail.Orthodox: Eastern Orthodoxy proclaims that Christ died for the life of the whole world, embracing all humanity without reserve. Yet the East frames the cross less as a legal payment satisfying divine justice and more within the drama of Pascha: Christ the Victor trampling down death by death, healing wounded human nature and reopening communion with God. These images are therapeutic and cosmic rather than forensic, so questions about the atonement's precise extent feel foreign to a tradition speaking of Christ restoring all things. Salvation is offered to everyone and unfolds as theosis.
detail.Anglican: Anglicanism confesses with its formularies that Christ made a full and sufficient sacrifice for the sins of the whole world, language echoed in the Thirty-nine Articles and the Book of Common Prayer. Prizing comprehensiveness, it leaves room for a genuine range: some read the cross in a more Reformed, particular key, while many others, shaped by the prayer book's universal offer, hold that Christ died for all. What unites them is that the sacrifice is sufficient for everyone and freely set before all, a mystery the Church prefers to pray rather than legislate.
detail.Lutheran: Lutheran theology holds that Christ died for all people universally, a scope the Book of Concord affirms without hedging, so the gospel promise may be preached to every sinner as genuinely for them. The decisive category is justification through faith alone: the atonement is objectively complete and universal in reach, yet grasped only where the Spirit works faith through Word and sacrament. Lutherans resist limiting Christ's death, insisting the fault for anyone's loss lies with unbelief, not a narrowed intention in God. This lets the preacher tell each hearer that Christ died for you.
detail.Wesleyan/Methodist: The Wesleyan conviction is that Christ tasted death for everyone, an unlimited atonement flowing from a love that excludes no one from the offer of salvation. Wesley preached that the same grace which purchased redemption for all also goes before all as prevenient grace, awakening even the resistant sinner and making a real response possible. This universal scope is no promise that all are automatically saved; the gift must still be received by faith. For Methodists the breadth of the cross is the heart of the good news.
detail.Reformed: In the Reformed confession the atonement is particular, or definite: Christ died to secure the salvation of the elect, not merely to make salvation possible for everyone. Following the Canons of Dort and the Westminster Standards, it stresses that the cross accomplished what it intended, purchasing every saving benefit for those given to Christ, so none for whom he died is finally lost. Reformed teachers still affirm the death is of infinite worth, sufficient for all, and freely offered to every hearer. The particularity lies in God's redemptive purpose, not the sacrifice's value.
detail.Baptist: The Baptist family genuinely divides here, which is why the cell reads "varies." General Baptists, in the Arminian stream, have historically taught a general atonement in which Christ died for all people, while Particular Baptists confess a definite atonement for the elect in the Reformed line that gave them their name. Most congregations fall somewhere along that spectrum, and documents like the Baptist Faith and Message affirm that salvation is offered to all who come. The extent of the atonement remains one of the family's liveliest internal debates over Calvinism.
detail.Pentecostal: Pentecostal churches overwhelmingly teach an unlimited atonement, proclaiming Christ died for everyone and salvation is freely available to whosoever will, reflecting their revivalist, Wesleyan-Holiness roots. Distinctively, much of the movement also locates healing in the atonement, holding that his suffering provides not only forgiveness of sin but healing of the body, drawn from Isaiah 53 and Matthew 8. Pentecostals differ on how fully to press that claim, from those expecting healing as a present covenant right to those receiving it as God's sovereign gift. They share a confidence that the cross reaches the whole person.

cells.Catholic: by grace through faith working in love; righteousness infused, not only declared
cells.Orthodox: framed as healing and participation more than a courtroom verdict
cells.Anglican: by faith (Article XI), rooted in the Reformation
cells.Lutheran: by faith alone; imputed righteousness, its central article
cells.Wesleyan/Methodist: by faith alone, but joined to real inward change
cells.Baptist: by faith alone; forensic and imputed
cells.Pentecostal: by faith; a genuine conversion and new birth

detail.Catholic: Catholic teaching, framed at Trent and restated in the Catechism, holds that justification is a real interior transformation, not merely a change in God's ledger: the grace won by Christ, poured into the heart, cleanses sin and makes the sinner truly righteous. It begins in the unmerited grace of Baptism and is received through a faith working through love, so that human cooperation is itself God's gift, never a boast. Because this righteousness is infused, not only imputed, it can grow, be lost through grave sin, and be restored.
detail.Orthodox: The Orthodox East rarely makes justification the organizing center of salvation, preferring the larger language of healing, union, and theosis — the sinner not so much acquitted as restored to the divine life. Where the West reasons in the categories of a courtroom, the East reads Paul through the Greek Fathers as a physician's account: sin is a sickness from which Christ rescues us, drawing us into his own righteousness. Faith and works are never weighed against each other, for both belong to the one movement of synergy.
detail.Anglican: Article XI of the Thirty-nine Articles states the Reformation conviction plainly: we are accounted righteous before God only for the merit of Christ by faith, not for our works — a doctrine it calls most wholesome and full of comfort. It points beyond itself to the Homily of Justification, anchoring Anglican teaching in Cranmer's Reformed inheritance. Yet it holds this together with a strong doctrine of sanctification, so saving faith is never bare assent but living trust. Accents vary across the Communion, some nearer Geneva, others more catholic, but the forensic core remains common ground.
detail.Lutheran: For Lutherans this is the article by which the Church stands or falls. Justification is by grace alone, through faith alone, on account of Christ alone: God declares the ungodly righteous not for any quality within them but because Christ's righteousness is imputed to the sinner who trusts the promise. The Book of Concord insists this verdict is entirely forensic, a real acquittal in heaven's court, so the believer is at once righteous and still a sinner — simul iustus et peccator. Good works follow as fruit but are excluded from the cause.
detail.Wesleyan/Methodist: Wesley received justification by faith alone from the Reformers and never softened it: it is God's merciful act of pardon, accepting us for the sake of Christ's atoning work and not for anything of our own. But he refused to let it stand alone, insisting that the moment God justifies the sinner he also begins the new birth, so Christ's imputed righteousness and a real imparted change go together. Justification is what God does for us; regeneration and sanctification are what he works in us — and Wesley would not divorce them.
detail.Reformed: The Reformed confessions, Westminster among them, define justification as a purely forensic act in which God pardons sin and accepts the sinner as righteous, resting on the righteousness of Christ imputed and received by faith alone. Faith is not itself the ground but the empty hand that lays hold of Christ, and even that faith is God's gift, so the whole transaction excludes human merit. Reformed theology carefully distinguishes justification from sanctification: the first a completed declaration, the second the renewal that follows. Good works are the evidence of living faith, never its foundation.
detail.Baptist: Across the Baptist family the doctrine is broadly shared: justification is God's gracious, forensic declaration that the believing sinner is righteous, grounded in Christ's imputed righteousness and received through faith alone, as the Baptist Faith and Message and the older Second London Confession affirm. Because Baptists prize personal response, this pardon is tied to a conscious, believing trust in Christ. The family differs less on justification than on its frame — Particular (Reformed) and General (Arminian) streams part over election and the atonement's extent — yet on its forensic, imputed character there is broad consensus.
detail.Pentecostal: Pentecostals stand within the wider evangelical stream and confess justification by grace through faith, God freely forgiving and accepting the sinner on the basis of Christ's finished work. Their characteristic emphasis, inherited from the movement's Wesleyan holiness roots, falls on this as a genuine conversion — a new birth a person knows and can testify to, not a bare legal formula. Justification, regeneration, and the start of Spirit-filled life belong together in their experience. The family ranges from Wesleyan bodies stressing a subsequent work of sanctification to Finished Work streams nearer the Reformed reading.

cells.Catholic: growth in infused grace, aided by the sacraments
cells.Orthodox: theosis: being made, by grace, a partaker of the divine nature
cells.Anglican: growth in grace through word and sacrament
cells.Lutheran: simul justus et peccator; growth by returning to baptism
cells.Wesleyan/Methodist: central; entire sanctification is possible — 'Christian perfection' meaning perfect love, not sinlessness
cells.Reformed: gradual; never completed in this life
cells.Baptist: gradual growth in the Christian life
cells.Pentecostal: holiness plus Spirit baptism as empowerment; sometimes a second work

detail.Catholic: Catholic teaching holds that sanctification is real inner transformation: at baptism God pours sanctifying grace into the soul, and that grace grows across a lifetime as the believer cooperates with it. The sacraments are its ordinary channels — the Eucharist nourishes charity, penance restores it when grave sin has driven it out — so holiness is neither a bare legal verdict nor mere human effort but grace working within. Trent and the Catechism insist that justification truly makes us righteous, not merely declares us so, and that this righteousness deepens through faith working in love.
detail.Orthodox: The Eastern churches frame the whole Christian life as theosis, or deification: by grace the believer becomes, as Second Peter says, a partaker of the divine nature. This is not absorption into God's essence, which remains forever unknowable, but real participation in his uncreated energies, so that one is progressively filled with divine life while remaining fully human. The Fathers put it starkly — God became human that the human might be made god by grace — and this unfolds through synergy, the Spirit and the free person cooperating in prayer, sacrament, and ascetic struggle.
detail.Anglican: Anglican theology treats sanctification as growth in grace nourished by word and sacrament within the ordered life of the Church. The Thirty-nine Articles affirm that good works are the fruit of a living faith and follow after justification, so holiness is evidence of grace already given, not its purchase price — a pattern the collects of the Book of Common Prayer form in practice. The tradition ranges from a Reformed accent on mortifying sin to those of the Oxford Movement who recover a stronger sacramental sense, yet the shape is one: means of grace faithfully used.
detail.Lutheran: Lutherans confess the believer as simul justus et peccator — at once wholly righteous in Christ and still a sinner in himself — so sanctification is not a climb out of that tension but life lived within it. Because justification is complete and received by faith alone, holiness flows from it as fruit rather than back into one's standing before God, an order the Formula of Concord guards. Growth comes through daily returning to baptism, which Luther called the drowning of the old self and the rising of the new, while assurance looks back to Christ.
detail.Wesleyan/Methodist: For Wesley sanctification is the heart of the gospel: the same grace that pardons also heals, and God intends actually to make us holy, not merely to count us so. He taught that entire sanctification, or Christian perfection, is possible in this life — but he meant perfect love, a heart so filled with love for God and neighbor that sin no longer reigns, not a flawless existence. Wesley freely granted that the perfected still err and depend on Christ; holiness is dynamic, often crowned by a definite second work of grace yet sustained only by faith.
detail.Reformed: Reformed theology teaches that sanctification is real and Spirit-wrought yet gradual, a lifelong mortifying of sin and quickening to righteousness never completed this side of glory. The Westminster Confession carefully distinguishes it from justification: the latter is a finished, once-for-all declaration, while sanctification is a progressive work in which even the regenerate still find remaining corruption warring against the Spirit. Because it rests on union with Christ rather than human achievement, believers can neither fall wholly away nor reach perfection here; their assurance rests on grace, not attainment, and good works are its fruit.
detail.Baptist: Baptists broadly understand sanctification as gradual growth in Christlikeness, the ongoing work of the Spirit following conversion, which the Baptist Faith and Message calls the process by which the redeemed are set apart and moved toward moral maturity. Yet the family genuinely ranges: many congregations shaped by Reformed convictions frame it as lifelong mortification never completed here, while others touched by Wesleyan-Holiness currents hold out fuller victory over sin or a definite second experience. What unites them is the conviction that a saved life will visibly bear fruit, worked by grace.
detail.Pentecostal: Pentecostals share the broader conviction that the Spirit sanctifies the believer, but add a stress on empowerment: baptism in the Holy Spirit, often evidenced by speaking in tongues, equips the Christian for holy living and witness. Here the family divides. Wesleyan-Holiness Pentecostals, such as the Church of God, teach a second work of grace that cleanses the heart, then Spirit baptism as a third experience; finished-work Pentecostals in the Assemblies of God line hold sanctification progressive from conversion and Spirit baptism an endowment of power, not cleansing. Either way, the Spirit purifies and equips.

cells.Catholic: yes: grace can be lost by mortal sin and restored through penance
cells.Orthodox: salvation is an ongoing synergy; one can turn away
cells.Anglican: the Articles do not require eternal security; a range is held
cells.Wesleyan/Methodist: yes: salvation can be forfeited; assurance by the Spirit's witness
cells.Reformed: no: the elect persevere and cannot finally fall
cells.Baptist: commonly no: once saved, always saved (eternal security)
cells.Pentecostal: commonly yes: one can fall away (Arminian majority)

detail.Catholic: The Catholic Church teaches that justifying grace, received in baptism, is a real sharing in God's life that can be forfeited by mortal sin — a grave act done with full knowledge and deliberate consent that severs charity and turns the heart from God. The Council of Trent condemned any claim to inviolable assurance, insisting that the justified must persevere with humble hope rather than presumption. Yet the loss is never final while life remains: the sacrament of Penance restores the grace that sin destroyed, which the tradition calls a second plank after shipwreck.
detail.Orthodox: For the Orthodox, salvation is not a verdict secured once but a lifelong healing — theosis, the soul's growing participation in God — worked by the synergy of divine grace and human freedom. Because the will remains genuinely free, a person can cool, resist, or turn away, so the Church prays constantly for a Christian ending and perseverance to the end. This is not an anxious tallying of merits but the language of a relationship kept alive, like a lamp that needs tending; falling is real, yet so is repentance.
detail.Anglican: Anglicanism deliberately leaves this question open, and the Thirty-nine Articles reflect that breadth. Article XVI grants that those who have received the Holy Spirit may depart from grace and fall into sin, yet may rise again by repentance — cutting against any claim of automatic security. Article XVII, on predestination, offers the elect real comfort while warning that its misuse breeds either presumption or despair. The result is a communion holding Reformed and more sacramental instincts side by side, so a faithful Anglican may lean toward perseverance or toward conditional grace without leaving the fold.
detail.Lutheran: Lutherans confess that faith is God's gift, sustained by Word and Sacrament, yet they teach plainly that a believer can fall away — grieving the Spirit, shipwrecking faith, and losing the salvation once held. The Formula of Concord rejects any security that makes the Christian careless, while equally rejecting the notion that we save ourselves by clinging on. Assurance rests not in the strength of my grip but in the promise attached to baptism and absolution, to which the fallen are always invited to return.
detail.Wesleyan/Methodist: Wesley taught that grace can be genuinely forfeited: a person truly justified may, by persistent unbelief or willful sin, make shipwreck of faith and fall from the favor of God. This is no counsel of dread, for the same Spirit who saves also witnesses with our spirit that we are God's children, granting a present, felt assurance rather than a bare inference. His hope of Christian perfection — being made perfect in love — meant not sinlessness but a heart wholly given to God, still growing, still dependent on grace.
detail.Reformed: The Reformed confessions teach the perseverance of the saints: those whom God effectually calls and justifies can neither totally nor finally fall away, since their standing rests on God's unchangeable election, Christ's continuing intercession, and the Spirit's indwelling rather than their own constancy. The Westminster Confession and the Canons of Dort grant that true believers may fall into grievous sin and lose the sense of God's favor for a time, yet they will be renewed and preserved to the end. This is no license for carelessness, so a fruitless life gives no ground for comfort.
detail.Baptist: The Baptist family leans strongly toward eternal security — the conviction, expressed in the Baptist Faith and Message, that those truly regenerated by God's Spirit will never finally fall away — though the reasoning splits along older lines. Particular (Reformed) Baptists ground it in the perseverance of the elect, while many others hold a broader "once saved, always saved" rooted in the finished work of Christ and the permanence of the new birth. Both insist that genuine faith bears fruit, while a minority in the General (Arminian) stream allows that apostasy is possible.
detail.Pentecostal: Most Pentecostals stand in the Arminian and Wesleyan-Holiness stream, teaching that salvation is received and kept through living faith, and that a believer who deliberately turns from Christ can fall away. Bodies such as the Assemblies of God frame this as a relationship to be maintained, not a transaction that cannot be undone, so backsliding is real and repentance is the way back. Yet a significant minority, shaped by Reformed or Baptist roots, do hold eternal security, so the conditional view predominates without being universal.

cells.Catholic: transubstantiation: the elements become Christ's body and blood
cells.Orthodox: a true mystery: the real presence affirmed, the how left unexplained
cells.Anglican: real spiritual presence; the tradition ranges from memorial to high
cells.Lutheran: real presence in, with, and under the elements (sacramental union)
cells.Wesleyan/Methodist: spiritual real presence; a means of grace at an open table
cells.Reformed: spiritual real presence, received by faith
cells.Baptist: a memorial; the elements are symbols (an ordinance)
cells.Pentecostal: an ordinance; the elements are symbolic

detail.Catholic: The Catholic Church confesses transubstantiation: at the priest's consecration the whole substance of the bread and wine is converted into Christ's body and blood, while the sensible appearances — taste, color, texture — remain. Following Trent and the Catechism, Christ is held to be truly, really, and substantially present, wholly under each species and in every fragment, which is why the reserved sacrament is adored. The point is not a chemical change or that Christ is crushed anew; the Aristotelian language of substance and accident is meant to guard a real conversion that eludes the senses.
detail.Orthodox: The Orthodox East affirms without hesitation that the bread and wine truly become the body and blood of Christ, yet it deliberately declines to define the manner. The change is real, effected by the descent of the Holy Spirit invoked in the epiclesis, and the gifts are venerated as Christ himself; but Orthodoxy treats the Eucharist as mystery in the deepest sense, resisting the scholastic machinery of substance and accident as an over-explanation. Some later Greek confessions did borrow the word transubstantiation, but the prevailing mind of the tradition is content to say that the how belongs to God, and to receive rather than dissect.
detail.Anglican: Anglicanism confesses a real spiritual presence: the Thirty-nine Articles reject transubstantiation as repugnant to the plain words of Scripture, yet insist the Supper is no bare sign but a true partaking of Christ's body and blood, received by faith in a heavenly and spiritual manner. Within that frame the tradition genuinely ranges — the memorialist end reads Cranmer's language receptionistically, while the high-church and Anglo-Catholic end, recovered by the Oxford Movement, approaches something near the Catholic understanding with candles, reservation, and adoration. What holds the poles together is the refusal to define the mechanism too tightly, letting the Prayer Book's worship carry what argument cannot settle.
detail.Lutheran: Lutherans confess the real presence in, with, and under the bread and wine — the sacramental union — so that communicants truly receive Christ's body and blood, given and shed for the forgiveness of sins. The Book of Concord takes Christ's words "this is my body" at face value, but declines the Catholic explanation: the bread remains bread and is not converted, for the two realities are united without either being destroyed. This is often miscalled consubstantiation, a label Lutherans themselves reject as a rival theory smuggled in; the confession is not a philosophy of how, but a stubborn fidelity to the promise attached to the words.
detail.Wesleyan/Methodist: For Wesley the Lord's Supper is a means of grace, not a mere remembrance: Christ is spiritually and really present to the faithful communicant, and the sacrament conveys the grace it signifies, even drawing seekers toward conversion. He called it a converting as well as a confirming ordinance, which grounds the open table — the invitation extends to all who seek the Lord, not only the already-assured. Methodists decline to specify the manner of presence, following the hymn's counsel to leave the how to omnipotence; what matters is that grace is genuinely given, and that no earnest heart is turned away.
detail.Reformed: Following Calvin and the Westminster Standards, the Reformed tradition teaches a real spiritual presence received by faith: the believer truly feeds on Christ's body and blood, not bodily with the mouth but by the Spirit, who lifts the communicant to the ascended Christ in heaven. The elements remain bread and wine, and the wicked who partake receive only the signs, since the gift is grasped by faith alone. This is a deliberate middle path, rejecting both a bodily presence in the elements and a bare memorial; sign and thing signified are distinct yet truly joined.
detail.Baptist: Baptists generally hold the Supper as an ordinance — a memorial in which the bread and cup are symbols proclaiming Christ's death until he comes, obeyed because he commanded it, not because the elements convey grace. This memorialist reading, shaped by Zwingli, dominates the family and is voiced in the Baptist Faith & Message, yet it is not the whole story: older confessions like the Second London speak of the faithful spiritually receiving Christ, nearer a Reformed sense. So the poles run from plain remembrance to real spiritual feeding, most congregations resting at the memorial end.
detail.Pentecostal: Pentecostals typically observe the Lord's Supper as an ordinance, a symbolic act of obedience in which the bread and cup memorialize Christ's broken body and shed blood and proclaim his return. Rooted largely in the free-church and Baptist stream, the movement reads the elements as signs rather than bearers of Christ's substance, emphasizing personal faith and the Spirit's immediate work in the gathered church. Yet the practice is rarely cold or bare: many congregations expect the Table to be an occasion for healing, examination, and renewal, so that while the theology is symbolic, the encounter with the living Christ through the Spirit is anticipated as genuinely powerful.

cells.Catholic: a regenerating sacrament; infants are baptized
cells.Orthodox: a regenerating mystery; infants baptized and chrismated
cells.Anglican: a regenerating sign; infants are baptized
cells.Lutheran: a regenerating means of grace; infants are baptized
cells.Wesleyan/Methodist: a means of grace; infants baptized, but not automatic
cells.Reformed: a covenant sign; infants are baptized
cells.Baptist: believer's baptism only; a symbol, by immersion
cells.Pentecostal: believer's baptism, plus a distinct baptism in the Spirit

detail.Catholic: The Catholic Church teaches that baptism is the sacrament that accomplishes what it signifies: it washes away sin, infuses sanctifying grace, and incorporates the recipient into Christ and the Church. Because this grace is God's own work, not a reward for understanding, the Church baptizes infants, trusting that regeneration precedes and enables a lifetime of faith. The Catechism calls it the gateway to the sacraments and teaches that its permanent character means baptism is never repeated. It is necessary as the ordinary means Christ gave, though the Church entrusts the unbaptized to God's mercy.
detail.Orthodox: For the Orthodox Church baptism is a genuine mystery, not merely a picture: through triple immersion the person dies and rises with Christ, is cleansed, and is born anew into the life of the Trinity. The East never separates new birth from the gift of the Spirit, so infants are baptized and immediately chrismated and given the Eucharist, receiving full membership at once. The emphasis falls less on erasing legal guilt than on healing human nature and beginning its participation in the divine life, what the tradition calls theosis.
detail.Anglican: Anglicanism confesses baptism as an effectual sign of grace, not a bare token: the Thirty-nine Articles call it a sign of regeneration by which we are grafted into the Church, and the Book of Common Prayer speaks of the baptized child as regenerate. Infants are baptized as they are brought within the covenant community. Within this shared liturgy the family holds a real range: some read the prayer book as teaching that grace is truly conveyed in the act, while others hold the new birth is pledged and received by faith rather than automatically bestowed.
detail.Lutheran: Lutherans hold that baptism is a true means of grace, an instrument through which the Holy Spirit works faith and delivers the forgiveness, life, and salvation won by Christ. As Luther's Small Catechism insists, it is not the water alone but the water bound to God's Word and promise that saves, received by faith. Infants are baptized because they too can be given faith by the Spirit, the gift depending on God's action rather than the recipient's decision. From the LCMS to the ELCA, Lutherans point back to their baptism for daily assurance.
detail.Wesleyan/Methodist: In the Wesleyan tradition baptism is a means of grace, a sign and seal of the new covenant through which God works, and infants are baptized because prevenient grace is already reaching toward them before they can respond. Yet Methodists resist treating it as automatic or magical: the grace conveyed must still be personally owned in faith as the child grows. Wesley honored his church's baptismal regeneration language while insisting the baptized still need the new birth of a living, converting faith. Baptism thus initiates and nurtures rather than finishing the work.
detail.Reformed: The Reformed churches understand baptism as a sign and seal of the covenant of grace, marking a person as belonging to God's covenant people much as circumcision once did in Israel. Infants of believers are baptized because the covenant promise embraces households, not because the water automatically regenerates; the Westminster Confession denies that grace is so tied to the sacrament that all who are baptized are saved. Efficacy belongs to the Spirit, who applies the benefit in God's own timing, so the sign points to a reality it does not mechanically produce.
detail.Baptist: Baptists confess believer's baptism alone: the ordinance is for those who have personally repented and trusted Christ, administered by full immersion as a picture of dying and rising with him. Because they read baptism as an outward symbol and public confession rather than a channel of saving grace, they decline to baptize infants, joining baptism to a professed faith the candidate can own. Within the family, from the Southern Baptist Convention to the Progressive National Baptist Convention, this insistence on a credible profession and immersion is the common ground even where other convictions differ.
detail.Pentecostal: Pentecostals practice believer's baptism in water by immersion, understanding it as an act of obedience and public witness for those who have already trusted Christ. What distinguishes the tradition is that it also proclaims a further, separate experience, the baptism in the Holy Spirit, an empowering for witness often evidenced, in classical bodies like the Assemblies of God, by speaking in tongues. These are held to be two distinct baptisms, in water and in the Spirit, not confused with conversion. Some streams treat tongues as the initial evidence; others describe Spirit baptism more broadly.

cells.Catholic: episcopal and hierarchical: bishops in apostolic succession, in communion with the Pope
cells.Orthodox: bishops in apostolic succession; a communion of self-governing churches, led conciliarly
cells.Anglican: episcopal: bishops, priests, and deacons, with governance shared through synods
cells.Lutheran: varies: some churches keep bishops, others are led by synods and elected presidents
cells.Wesleyan/Methodist: connectional: bishops and annual conferences appoint clergy and order the church's life
cells.Reformed: presbyterian: rule by elders through graded courts — session, presbytery, assembly
cells.Baptist: congregational: each local church is self-governing and autonomous under Christ
cells.Pentecostal: varies: from congregational to overseer-led, often with district and general councils

detail.Catholic: Catholic teaching holds that Christ endowed the Church with a hierarchical structure, governed by the college of bishops who succeed the apostles, in communion with the bishop of Rome as Peter's successor. The Second Vatican Council's Lumen Gentium describes this collegiality: bishops govern their local churches yet share responsibility for the whole, united with the Pope. This is sacramental order, not mere administration, since episcopal consecration confers the fullness of Holy Orders. A misreading imagines the Pope as an absolute monarch, when the Church rather understands him as the source of the bishops' unity.
detail.Orthodox: The Orthodox Church understands itself as a communion of self-governing (autocephalous) churches, each led by bishops in apostolic succession and bound together not by a single earthly head but by shared faith, sacraments, and councils. Every bishop is equal in order, though some hold an honored primacy; the Ecumenical Patriarch of Constantinople is first among equals, with no jurisdiction over the rest. Authority is exercised conciliarly, following the early councils, where the whole body discerns the truth together. Orthodoxy resists the idea of a universal pastor: no bishop stands above the council of his brothers.
detail.Anglican: Anglicanism retains the historic threefold ministry of bishops, priests, and deacons, understanding episcopacy as an inheritance from the ancient Church rather than an invention of the Reformation, as the Preface to the Ordinal attests. Yet governance is shared: bishops lead, but decisions are made synodically, with clergy and laity deliberating together in diocesan and general synods. This blend of episcopal oversight and representative counsel reflects the Anglican instinct for dispersed authority. Across the global Communion the balance varies, and Anglicans hold together by bonds of affection and shared worship more than by central legislative power.
detail.Lutheran: Lutherans left church government to freedom rather than divine command, holding that the Gospel prescribes no single polity, so the family genuinely varies. The Augsburg Confession treats the office of ministry as essential but the form of oversight as adiaphora, a thing indifferent. Thus some churches keep bishops in historic succession, as in Scandinavia; the LCMS is led by elected district and synod presidents; and the ELCA has bishops without holding succession necessary. What unites them is not polity but the pure preaching of the Word and right administration of the sacraments.
detail.Wesleyan/Methodist: Methodism is connectional: no congregation stands alone, but all are woven together through annual conferences and bishops who, in the episcopal Methodist tradition, appoint clergy and order the church's shared life. This flows from Wesley, who governed the early societies through conference and itinerancy, sending preachers where they were needed. The bishop's authority is pastoral and administrative, not a separate order of ministry: Methodists consecrate bishops from among the elders. The genius of connection is mutual accountability: ministry belongs to the whole body moving together, not to the isolated local church.
detail.Reformed: The Reformed churches are governed presbyterially, by assemblies of elders rather than a single bishop, convinced that Christ rules his Church through a plurality of ordained leaders. The Westminster form arranges these in graded courts: the session governs the local congregation, the presbytery a region, and the general assembly the whole. Ministers and ruling elders sit as equals in these courts, so authority ascends through shared deliberation rather than descending from a hierarch. Bodies like the PCA and PCUSA share this structure despite sharp differences elsewhere, for presbyterian polity is the family's defining mark.
detail.Baptist: Baptists are congregational: each local church is self-governing and autonomous, answerable directly to Christ as its only head, calling its own ministers without a higher court over it. The Baptist Faith and Message and the Second London Confession ground this in the priesthood of all believers and the competence of the gathered church under Scripture. The autonomy is not isolation: Baptists cooperate through associations and conventions — the SBC, the Cooperative Baptist Fellowship, the Progressive National Baptist Convention — none holding authority over any congregation. Autonomy under Christ, joined to voluntary cooperation, is the enduring Baptist pattern.
detail.Pentecostal: Pentecostal church government genuinely varies, ranging from congregational independence to strongly overseer-led structures, reflecting the movement's growth from many separate revivals. Some bodies, like the Assemblies of God, are cooperative, joining autonomous local churches through district and general councils that credential ministers and coordinate mission. Others, like the Church of God in Christ or the Foursquare Church, are more episcopal, with bishops or overseers exercising real oversight over congregations. Across this range, Pentecostals commonly hold that true authority rests in the Spirit's leading, so structures serve his work rather than stand as ends in themselves.

cells.Catholic: ordination is reserved to men; women serve widely but are not ordained priests
cells.Orthodox: the priesthood is male; women serve in monastic, teaching, and historic diaconal roles
cells.Anglican: varies: the Episcopal Church ordains women as priests and bishops; some Anglican bodies do not
cells.Lutheran: varies: the ELCA ordains women; the LCMS reserves the pastoral office to men
cells.Wesleyan/Methodist: women are ordained to every office, including bishop, with full clergy rights
cells.Reformed: varies: the PCUSA ordains women; the PCA and confessional bodies ordain only men
cells.Baptist: varies: many Baptists ordain women; the SBC reserves the office of pastor for men
cells.Pentecostal: often open to women preaching and ordination, a mark since its earliest days

detail.Catholic: The Catholic Church teaches that ordination to the priesthood is reserved to men, rooting this not in any judgment about women's worth but in fidelity to Christ, who chose men as his apostles, and in the priest acting in persona Christi. John Paul II's Ordinatio Sacerdotalis presented this as something the Church has no authority to change, not merely a custom. Yet women are far from barred from ministry: they serve as theologians, chancellors, catechists, and chaplains, and the Church holds up Mary and the women Doctors as its highest models of discipleship.
detail.Orthodox: Orthodoxy holds that the priesthood and episcopate are male, understanding the bishop and presbyter to image Christ at the altar within an unbroken apostolic tradition the Church receives rather than legislates. This is framed as continuity with the Fathers, not a low estimate of women, whom Orthodoxy venerates supremely in the Theotokos and a great cloud of women saints and monastic mothers. Women have long served as teachers, iconographers, and abbesses, and in the ancient order of deaconess, whose restoration several churches have studied.
detail.Anglican: Anglicanism genuinely varies, since the Communion vests authority in provinces that have decided differently while sharing the Thirty-nine Articles and the historic threefold order. The Episcopal Church and many provinces ordain women as deacons, priests, and bishops, reasoning that nothing in Scripture or the Articles finally bars it and that the Spirit's gifts fall without respect to sex. Other bodies, including parts of the Global South and continuing churches, ordain only men, some citing headship. Many provinces keep conscience provisions so both convictions coexist.
detail.Lutheran: Lutherans divide here, though all appeal to the same Book of Concord and the priesthood of all believers. The Evangelical Lutheran Church in America ordains women as pastors and bishops, judging the office of Word and Sacrament open to all whom the Church rightly calls. The Lutheran Church–Missouri Synod reserves the pastoral office to men, reading Paul's instructions in First Corinthians and First Timothy as an abiding apostolic order rather than a cultural accommodation. Other ministries remain open to women, so the line falls specifically at the pastoral office.
detail.Wesleyan/Methodist: The Wesleyan tradition ordains women to every office, including bishop, with full clergy rights and no ceiling on where the call may lead. Wesley himself authorized women to preach when he saw the Spirit's fruit on their labor, and the movement holds that if God calls and gifts a woman, the Church dares not silence her. Methodists read the ministering women of the Gospels, Phoebe the deacon, and Paul's word that in Christ there is neither male nor female as warrant for full inclusion, testing a vocation by its fruit.
detail.Reformed: The Reformed family is divided here, with both poles claiming careful fidelity to Scripture and the Westminster Standards. The Presbyterian Church (USA) and kindred bodies ordain women as deacons, elders, and ministers of Word and Sacrament, holding that the Spirit distributes office-bearing gifts without regard to sex. Confessional churches such as the Presbyterian Church in America and the Orthodox Presbyterian Church ordain only men, reading Paul on eldership and male headship as a permanent creational order. Some still commission women to substantial diaconal and teaching work.
detail.Baptist: The Baptist family is the most divided of all here, and congregational polity means each church finally decides for itself. Bodies such as the Cooperative Baptist Fellowship, the Alliance of Baptists, and the Progressive National Baptist Convention ordain women freely, including as senior pastors, appealing to soul competency and the priesthood of all believers. The Southern Baptist Convention, in its Baptist Faith and Message, reserves the office of pastor for men while affirming women in other ministries. Between these poles lie many independent Baptists, so no single answer exists.
detail.Pentecostal: Pentecostalism has been notably open to women from its earliest days, when the outpouring at Azusa Street was read through Joel's prophecy that sons and daughters alike would prophesy. Women such as Aimee Semple McPherson founded movements and pulpits, and bodies like the Assemblies of God and the Foursquare Church ordain women to preach and pastor. The stress on the Spirit's evident gifting has often outweighed formal objections, making a Spirit-anointed call the decisive credential. Still, some Pentecostal and Holiness bodies limit the highest governing office.

cells.Catholic: the charisms are real and continue, exercised under the Church's discernment
cells.Orthodox: the gifts continue, seen especially in the saints, elders, and the Church's life
cells.Anglican: a range, from cautious to the charismatic renewal welcomed in parts of the Communion
cells.Lutheran: the Spirit works through Word and sacrament; the sign gifts are held with caution
cells.Wesleyan/Methodist: open to the gifts, with love and holiness of life as their measure
cells.Reformed: commonly cessationist: the sign gifts authenticated the apostles and have ceased
cells.Baptist: commonly cessationist, though many are open but cautious about the sign gifts
cells.Pentecostal: the gifts are for today; tongues, prophecy, and healing mark the Spirit-filled life

detail.Catholic: The Catechism teaches that the Holy Spirit distributes charisms among the faithful of every rank, from extraordinary gifts to the simple and widely diffused graces that build up the Church. These are real and continue in every age, ordered always to charity and the common good rather than to private display. Because a genuine gift can be counterfeited or misused, discernment belongs finally to those who lead the Church, whose task is to test the spirits and hold fast to what is good. The charisms are welcomed as the Spirit's own gifts, neither quenched nor left ungoverned.
detail.Orthodox: For the Orthodox, the gifts of the Spirit are inseparable from the life of holiness, for the whole aim of the Christian life, as Saint Seraphim of Sarov taught, is the acquisition of the Holy Spirit. The charisms endure and are seen most clearly in the saints and the elders, in healing, discernment of hearts, and prophetic counsel, as fruit of ascetic purification and prayer rather than techniques sought for their own sake. The Church treasures these signs yet tests them soberly, wary of delusion, prizing humility and love above spectacular manifestation.
detail.Anglican: Anglicanism holds no single position here but a genuine range across the Communion. Its older temper, shaped by a reformed reading of Scripture and the Thirty-nine Articles, was cautious toward claims of extraordinary gifts, stressing Word, sacrament, and ordered ministry. Yet from the mid-twentieth century the charismatic renewal was warmly received in many parishes and provinces, and today Anglicans span the settled sacramental piety of some congregations and the Spirit-filled expectancy of others. The via media here is less a fixed doctrine than a shared willingness to hold both together in one Church.
detail.Lutheran: Lutherans confess that the Holy Spirit gives faith where and when he wills, yet does so through the means of grace, the preached Word and the sacraments, not apart from them. The Augsburg Confession's rejection of those who expect the Spirit without the external Word shapes a lasting caution toward the sign gifts, which are neither denied outright nor eagerly sought. The accent falls on the Spirit's sure and ordinary work: creating faith, forgiving sin, sustaining vocation in daily life. Bodies differ in temper, but the common instinct locates the Spirit's power in Christ delivered through Word and sacrament.
detail.Wesleyan/Methodist: Wesley was open to the Spirit's gifts and unwilling to consign them to the apostolic past, suspecting that they waned less from God's design than from the Church's coldness of love. Yet he measured every claimed gift by a surer standard: the fruit of the Spirit and holiness of heart and life, for love is the more excellent way that outlasts prophecy and tongues. Extraordinary gifts, in this reading, serve the ordinary end of sanctification and the building up of others, never self-display. Methodism thus welcomes the charisms while insisting that their proof is a life conformed to Christ in love.
detail.Reformed: The Reformed tradition has commonly held a cessationist view: the sign gifts, tongues, prophecy, and miraculous healing, served to authenticate the apostles and confirm the gospel as revelation was being given, and so ceased once their purpose was complete. With the canon of Scripture closed, the Spirit's ongoing work is bound to the Word he inspired, illuminating hearts rather than adding new revelation. This is not a denial that God still answers prayer or heals; it distinguishes providence from the office of a miracle-worker. The family is not uniform, and a continuationist current has grown among some Reformed evangelicals.
detail.Baptist: The Baptist family genuinely varies here, which is why no single line captures it. A strong strand is cessationist, teaching that the sign gifts confirmed the apostolic message and passed with the apostolic age, while many other Baptists remain open yet cautious, unwilling to forbid what Scripture describes but wary of excess and disorder. The Baptist Faith and Message affirms the Spirit's gifts for service without mandating any one theory of the miraculous, leaving congregations free under the authority of Scripture. Between careful cessationists and cautiously open churches lies most of the family, with charismatic Baptists at one edge.
detail.Pentecostal: Pentecostals confess that the gifts described in the New Testament are fully for today, poured out in the last days as at the first Pentecost. Many hold that baptism in the Holy Spirit, often evidenced by tongues, empowers believers for witness, and that prophecy, healing, and the whole array of charisms mark the Spirit-filled life. This is not a claim that every believer receives every gift, for the Spirit apportions as he wills, nor a preference for experience over Scripture. The gifts are gracious equipment for mission, tested by their conformity to Christ and the written Word.

cells.Catholic: one salvation history; the Church as the People of God, Israel's covenant never revoked
cells.Orthodox: one divine economy unfolding to Christ and fulfilled in the Church, read through the Fathers
cells.Anglican: one covenant of grace across both Testaments; the Church continuous with Israel
cells.Lutheran: law and gospel run through every age; one people saved by the promise in Christ
cells.Wesleyan/Methodist: one unfolding story of grace; the Church grafted into the covenant made with Israel
cells.Reformed: covenant theology: one covenant of grace administered differently through the ages
cells.Baptist: varies: from covenantal to dispensational, some sharply distinguishing Israel and the Church
cells.Pentecostal: often premillennial, expecting Christ's soon return; many hold a dispensational frame

detail.Catholic: Catholic teaching reads Scripture as one continuous salvation history, an economy of divine self-giving that reaches its center in Christ and continues in the Church. The Catechism, following Nostra Aetate and Lumen Gentium, insists that God's covenant with Israel was never revoked; the Church does not replace the Jewish people but is grafted onto the same root, the new People of God gathered from all nations. This heads off the old error of supersessionism: the older covenant is fulfilled, not annulled. History moves under Providence toward the fullness of the Kingdom, when God will be all in all.
detail.Orthodox: Orthodoxy speaks of the one divine economy — God's saving arrangement of all things — unfolding from creation through Israel to the Incarnation and continuing in the Church, the Body of Christ. Read through the Fathers, every stage is held together by the eternal Word, whose comings the prophets foreshadowed and whose Spirit now vivifies the sacramental life. The aim is not merely legal pardon but theosis, humanity's participation in the divine life, gathered up toward the age to come. Time is thus one movement of communion, recapitulated in Christ as Irenaeus taught, awaiting its consummation in the Kingdom.
detail.Anglican: Anglican formularies confess one covenant of grace running through both Testaments. Article VII of the Thirty-nine Articles states plainly that the Old and New Testaments are not contrary, for in both everlasting life is offered through Christ, the only Mediator; the fathers under the Law looked forward to the same promise we receive. So the Church stands continuous with Israel rather than displacing it, the same grace administered under changing forms. This guards against reading the Old Testament as a merely earthly bargain: ceremonies pass away, but the covenant of grace is one and abiding.
detail.Lutheran: For Lutherans the master key to Scripture is the distinction between law and gospel, and both words sound in every age. The law always accuses and drives to Christ; the gospel announces the promise, so that Abraham, David, and the believer today are saved by one and the same faith in the promised Seed. The Book of Concord teaches that this promise, given in Eden and renewed to the patriarchs, is fulfilled in Christ. History is thus the unfolding of a single promise, received always by grace through faith alone.
detail.Wesleyan/Methodist: Wesleyans read history as one unfolding story of grace, God patiently working from creation toward the restoration of all things. Grace comes in the same order in every age — prevenient going before, justifying pardoning, sanctifying healing — so the covenant with Israel is not cancelled but enlarged, the Gentiles grafted into the olive tree Paul describes. Wesley's optimism of grace expects that love will finally have its way in a redeemed people and world. This is no automatic march of progress, but the sovereign tenderness of a God who wills all to be saved.
detail.Reformed: Reformed theology frames the whole of history as covenant. Beneath the outward changes stands one covenant of grace, administered differently through the ages — promised to Adam, sealed with Abraham, tutored under Moses, and fulfilled in Christ — yet substantially the same throughout, as the Westminster Confession teaches. This is why the Reformed baptize infants and see the Church as the continuation of Israel's assembly rather than a wholly new people. The shared conviction is unity: not two peoples or two plans, but one gracious purpose worked out across time.
detail.Baptist: The Baptist family genuinely spans the spectrum here, which is why the cell reads "varies." Many Baptists are covenantal, seeing one people of God across the Testaments, while others hold a dispensational frame that distinguishes God's program for Israel from his program for the Church, sometimes sharply. The Baptist Faith & Message leaves the timetable open, affirming Christ's return and consummation without binding a single millennial scheme. Between the poles sits a large middle, so a congregation's view depends heavily on its own teachers rather than any denominational decree.
detail.Pentecostal: Pentecostal spirituality is shaped by lively expectation: the same Spirit poured out at Pentecost is poured out now in the last days, and Christ is coming soon. Many classical Pentecostals, following the Assemblies of God Statement of Fundamental Truths, hold a premillennial and often dispensational reading, anticipating the rapture, tribulation, and millennial reign. Yet the movement is not uniform — some read the Spirit's outpouring as inaugurating God's restoring work more broadly. What unites them is urgency: history is hastening toward its King, and the Spirit's present power is the down payment of that day.

cells.Catholic: the Mass at the center: liturgical, sacramental, ordered by the Church's calendar
cells.Orthodox: the Divine Liturgy: ancient, sung, iconographic, and unchanging in shape
cells.Anglican: ordered by the Book of Common Prayer, ranging from high ceremonial to low
cells.Lutheran: liturgical and hymn-rich, centered on the preached Word and the Sacrament
cells.Wesleyan/Methodist: a blend: ordered liturgy joined to revival warmth and Wesley's hymnody
cells.Reformed: Word-centered and orderly, historically plain, with the sermon at the center
cells.Baptist: free and informal, the sermon central; style ranges from traditional to contemporary
cells.Pentecostal: expressive and spontaneous: extended praise, testimony, and freedom for the Spirit

detail.Catholic: For Catholics the Mass is the center of worship because it is not merely a memorial but the re-presentation of Christ's one sacrifice, called by the Second Vatican Council the source and summit of the Christian life. Word and Eucharist form a single act, shaped by the liturgical year that walks the faithful through Advent, Lent, Easter, and Ordinary Time. Forms range from a quiet daily Mass to solemn high liturgy with incense and chant, and worship remains the ordered public prayer of the whole Body rather than a matter of private preference.
detail.Orthodox: Orthodox worship centers on the Divine Liturgy, chiefly that of St. John Chrysostom, understood as the Church's participation in the heavenly worship around God's throne. It is sung rather than spoken, densely scriptural, and surrounded by icons venerated as windows to Christ and the saints, never worshiped as idols. The ancient, stable shape is not dead repetition but a form meant to free the worshiper into encounter with the living God. Fasting seasons and the cycle of feasts give the year its rhythm, the whole offered as the joint work of clergy and people.
