detail.Anglican: Anglican worship is governed by common prayer, so that the Book of Common Prayer, not the preacher's mood, orders how a congregation gathers, confesses, hears Scripture, and receives Communion. Within that shared framework the family spans a genuine range: Anglo-Catholic parishes with vestments, incense, and a weekly Eucharist at one pole, and plainer evangelical or low-church settings centered on preaching at the other. This is not incoherence but the via media working as designed, holding reformed conviction and catholic order together. Scripture read in course and the daily offices of Morning and Evening Prayer form the backbone beneath every style.
detail.Lutheran: Lutheran worship keeps the historic liturgy because Luther reformed the Mass rather than discarding it, retaining its shape while placing the preached gospel at the heart. Word and Sacrament stand together as the means through which God actually delivers forgiveness, so the sermon proclaims Christ and the Supper gives him, and neither is treated as mere symbol. Congregational hymnody is a Lutheran hallmark, a way the whole assembly sings the faith it confesses. Practice ranges from the formal chanted services common in the LCMS to freer contemporary settings found in parts of the ELCA, but the Word-and-Sacrament center remains constant.
detail.Wesleyan/Methodist: Methodist worship is a deliberate blend, joining the ordered liturgy Wesley inherited from the Church of England to the warmth of the revival and the singing of Charles Wesley's hymns. Wesley never meant to leave sacrament behind for enthusiasm; he urged constant Communion even as he preached in fields and prized the heart strangely warmed. So a Methodist service can hold together a printed order, fervent hymnody, and extemporary prayer without contradiction. The aim throughout is that worship form disciples in holiness of heart and life, uniting what he called the twin means of grace, the instituted and the prudential.
detail.Reformed: Reformed worship is Word-centered and orderly, governed by the conviction that God is to be worshiped only as he has commanded, so read and preached Scripture holds the central place with the sermon as the high point. Historically this yielded a plain style with psalm-singing and simple sacraments, keeping focus on God's speech rather than human artistry. Confessions like Westminster and Heidelberg supply the backbone. Today the family ranges from traditions of exclusive psalmody to PCA and PCUSA congregations that welcome hymns and fuller liturgy, yet reverent order under the Word remains the common thread.
detail.Baptist: Baptist worship is typically free and informal, shaped by the conviction that each local congregation orders its own life under Christ's lordship without a prescribed liturgy, with the sermon carrying central weight as the exposition of Scripture. Beyond that shared instinct the family varies widely: some keep a traditional hymn-and-pulpit pattern while others are fully contemporary, and a Progressive National Baptist or historically Black congregation may feature call-and-response and rich gospel music foreign to a quieter rural chapel. Style is left to the congregation, so freedom, not any single form, is the family's signature here.
detail.Pentecostal: Pentecostal worship is expressive and spontaneous, giving the Spirit room to move through extended praise, testimony, and gifts like tongues, prophecy, and healing. Yet this openness is not disorder; classical Pentecostals cite Paul's counsel that all be done decently and in order, so freedom operates within a service that has shape and leadership. Practice ranges from the exuberant, drawn-out worship of independent and Apostolic assemblies to the more structured services of bodies like the Assemblies of God. What unites them is the expectation that the risen Christ is present and active, worshipers free to respond.

traditions.name: Eastern Orthodox
traditions.blurb: Patristic and liturgical; mystery, the Mysteries, and theosis. Practice sits beside Rome, but its participatory, apophatic spirit reaches toward Wesley.

traditions.name: Roman Catholic
traditions.blurb: Church, Tradition, and Magisterium together; seven sacraments; transubstantiation; grace mediated through the sacramental life.

traditions.name: Anglo-Catholic
traditions.blurb: The sacramental, apostolic wing of Anglicanism; high liturgy and Prayer Book piety.

traditions.name: Lutheran (LCMS)
traditions.blurb: Confessional Lutheranism; sola scriptura plus real presence, strongly liturgical and confessional.

traditions.name: Lutheran (ELCA)
traditions.blurb: Mainline Lutheranism; the same sacramental core as the LCMS, the mainline of the two major US Lutheran bodies.

traditions.name: Episcopal (US)
traditions.blurb: The US expression of Anglicanism; deeply sacramental and broadly inclusive.

traditions.name: Methodist / Wesleyan (UMC/GMC)
traditions.blurb: The via media: means of grace joined to revival warmth. Center of the field, which is why it can bridge in several directions.

traditions.name: Presbyterian (PCUSA)
traditions.blurb: Mainline Reformed; shares the Westminster heritage of the PCA, in the mainline branch.

traditions.blurb: The believers' church: discipleship, peace, and separation from the world; believer's baptism.

traditions.name: Pentecostal / charismatic (AoG)
traditions.blurb: Spirit baptism and the gifts; experiential and low-sacrament, with Wesleyan-holiness roots.

traditions.name: General / Free Will Baptist

traditions.name: Southern Baptist
traditions.blurb: The biblicist pole: Scripture alone, believer's baptism, symbolic ordinances, conversion-centered.

traditions.name: Independent Fundamental / fundamentalist

traditions.name: UCC / Unitarian
traditions.blurb: The tradition descended from New England Congregationalism; non-creedal and congregational, holding the broadest doctrinal latitude of the bodies shown here.

family_denominations.Anglican: the Episcopal Church, the Anglican Church in North America (ACNA), and the Church of England and wider Anglican Communion; at the edge, the United Church of Christ
family_denominations.Lutheran: the ELCA, the Lutheran Church–Missouri Synod (LCMS), the Wisconsin Synod (WELS), and the North American Lutheran Church
family_denominations.Wesleyan/Methodist: the United Methodist Church and the Global Methodist Church; the AME, AME Zion, and CME churches; the Church of the Nazarene, the Free Methodist Church, the Wesleyan Church, and the Salvation Army
family_denominations.Reformed: the PCUSA, the PCA, the EPC and OPC; the Reformed Church in America and the Christian Reformed Church; and the Congregational churches
family_denominations.Baptist: the Southern Baptist Convention, the National and Progressive National Baptist Conventions, American Baptist Churches USA, and the Free Will Baptists; the Churches of Christ; Mennonite and other Anabaptist churches; and many nondenominational and community churches
family_denominations.Pentecostal: the Assemblies of God, the Church of God in Christ (COGIC), the Church of God (Cleveland, TN), the Foursquare Church, and the wider charismatic movement
